coredata.dc:title: Cascade energy transfer endows phosphorescent carbon dots-based films with tunable and broadband afterglow for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting

Ding, Xiangbo
Cai, Lin
Jia, Yihan
Ran, Xia
Guo, Zhongzheng
Mo, Dan
Pan, Yatao
Guo, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their afterglow properties and promising potential in optical information and encryption fields. However, achieving tunable and broadband RTP CDs-based afterglow materials remains a great challenge for practical applications. Herein, we constructed broadly tunable afterglow CDs-based hybrid systems by employing cascade Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting. The as-prepared CDs composites exhibit excellent green RTP properties with a quantum yield of 21.7 % and a lifetime of 1.05 s. Combination of the RTP CDs as the energy donor and Sulforhodamine 101 (SR101), Rhodamine B or Rhodamine 6G as the acceptor in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, the afterglow spectrum of RTP CDs/dye/PVA film is markedly extended to the red region through an efficient triplet-singlet FRET. The sequential incorporation of SR101 and Oxazine 170 with RTP CDs further broadens the afterglow spectrum of CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA film covering from green to near-infrared regions due to the cascade relayed FRET. The flexible FRET configuration of highly emissive RTP CDs with various dye acceptors endows the CDs/dyes/PVA hybrid films with tunable color and broadband afterglow properties for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Resonance energy transfer
Tunable afterglow
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Anticounterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of preparation procedures of CDs-based films. (b) UV/Vis absorption (red line), steady-state emission (black line) and excitation (blue line) spectra of CD1 in the boric acid matrix, with the blank dotted line representing the Tauc plot of CD1. (c) Afterglow emission and excitation spectra at the emission of 525 nm of CD1 powder. (d) Phosphorescence decay of CD1 powder at 365 nm excitation. (e) Images of CD1, CD2, and CD3 powders under and after the excitation at 365 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Morphology and chemical structure characterization. (a) TEM and HR-TEM images, and size distribution of CD1. (b) FT-IR spectra of CD1, CD2, CD3 composites. (c) XPS survey scan of CD1 composite, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) B 1s spectra of CD1 composite.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of the CDs/PVA; absorption and fluorescence spectra of SR101 and Oxazine 170. (b) Schematic diagram of cascade FRET of CDs-based system. (c) Afterglow spectra of CDs/SR101/PVA films with varying SR101 contents under excitation of 365 nm. (d) Phosphorescence decay curves and corresponding fitted lines of CDs/SR101/PVA films at 525 nm with different SR101 dopants under 365 nm excitation. (e) Energy transfer efficiency between CDs and SR101 in CDs/SR101/PVA films with different concentrations of SR101.

Steady-state PL spectra (a–c) and time-resolved afterglow spectra with different delay times (d–f) of CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films with different content of Oxazine 170. (g) PL decay curves and corresponding fitted lines of CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films at 610 nm with varying contents of Oxazine 170. (h) FRET efficiencies between SR101 and Oxazine 170 from lifetime measurements. (i) CIE coordinates (x, y) calculated from the RTP spectra of CDs/SR101/PVA with varying SR101 contents, and CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films with varying Oxazine 170 contents. (j) Photographs of CDs/PVA, CDs/SR101/PVA, and CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films under/after UV excitations.

(a) Latent fingerprint detection using RTP CDs/PVA. (b) Illustration of multicolor CDs-based hybrids for multi-level anticounterfeiting. (c) Handwritten character images of CDs-based afterglow ink under/after the UV excitations.

head.ce:title: Cascade energy transfer endows phosphorescent carbon dots-based films with tunable and broadband afterglow for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Henan Key Laboratory of High Efficiency Energy Conversion Science and Technology, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of High Efficiency Energy Conversion Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Henan Key Laboratory of High Efficiency Energy Conversion Science and Technology, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Infrared Spectrum Measures and Applications, School of Physics, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Infrared Spectrum Measures and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Infrared Spectrum Measures and Applications, School of Physics, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cascade FRET-assisted phosphorescent CDs enable broadband tunable afterglow for advanced fingerprint detection and anti-counterfeiting.

Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their afterglow properties and promising potential in optical information and encryption fields. However, achieving tunable and broadband RTP CDs-based afterglow materials remains a great challenge for practical applications. Herein, we constructed broadly tunable afterglow CDs-based hybrid systems by employing cascade Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting. The as-prepared CDs composites exhibit excellent green RTP properties with a quantum yield of 21.7 % and a lifetime of 1.05 s. Combination of the RTP CDs as the energy donor and Sulforhodamine 101 (SR101), Rhodamine B or Rhodamine 6G as the acceptor in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, the afterglow spectrum of RTP CDs/dye/PVA film is markedly extended to the red region through an efficient triplet-singlet FRET. The sequential incorporation of SR101 and Oxazine 170 with RTP CDs further broadens the afterglow spectrum of CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA film covering from green to near-infrared regions due to the cascade relayed FRET. The flexible FRET configuration of highly emissive RTP CDs with various dye acceptors endows the CDs/dyes/PVA hybrid films with tunable color and broadband afterglow properties for fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, a unique category of photoluminescent materials, exhibit sustained afterglow emissions even without an excitation source. RTP materials can eliminate background interference, making them pivotal in biological fluorescence tagging and optical information storage. Traditional organic RTP materials are synthesized by fabricating specific structures, such as molecular clusters and host–guest systems [1,2], or by incorporating special functional groups, including heavy halogens [3] and aromatic aldehydes [4]. However, most of these RTP materials suffer from limitations such as short afterglow lifetimes, poor photostability, and intricate synthesis procedures, which substantially limit their practical application. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as a novel luminescent carbon nanomaterial have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent optical properties, high stability, and good biocompatibility [5–14]. Such advantages enable their potential applications in various fields such as optical fabrication [15,16], energy conversion [17], biomedicine [18–20], and information security [21,22]. Concurrently, the phosphorescence of CDs is commonly induced and intensified through heteroatom doping [23–26] or by incorporating CDs into a host matrix like organic compounds (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [27,28], cyanuric acid [29,30], etc.) and inorganic compounds (e.g., boric acid [31,32], hydroxy fluorides [33], silica [34,35], zeolites [36,37], etc.). For instance, Ai et al. developed four metal-free CDs by structural tailoring and achieved full-color high-brightness RTP by strengthening chemical encapsulation in urea, the maximum lifetime and quantum yield are 2141 ms and 56.55 %, respectively [38]. Ding et al. have reported a facile method for fabricating boron-doped CDs composites that can be tailored to emit long-lasting RTP across the full-color spectrum by pyrolyzing citric acid and boric acid in varying mass rations at different temperatures [39]. Liu et al. synthesized CDs exhibiting thermochromic afterglow (TCAG) and excitation-related afterglow color properties by heat-treating levofloxacin in melted boric acid. By adjusting the ratio of temperature-dependent TADF and phosphorescence, the CDs-inked paper TCAG exhibits a dynamic color change from blue at high temperature to yellow at low temperature [40].

In recent years, significant progress has been made in developing CDs-based RTP materials, with notable advancements in long afterglow lifetimes, multicolor emission, and dynamic response [41–45]. However, the RTP emission of CDs is typically confined in the spectral region below 630 nm. Precisely controlling the RTP properties of CDs-based systems for broad-spectrum emission is challenging due to their complex photoluminescence mechanism and the lack of effective strategies that can concurrently achieve long lifetime, high quantum efficiency, and manufacturability. Consequently, this limitation severely restricts their applications in anticounterfeiting and information identification. Sequential phosphorescence energy transfer (PRET) features a pronounced Stokes shift andoffers substantial potential across various applications [46–48]. For example, Zheng and coworkers reported a synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on FRET and TADF, successfully constructing a long-wavelength RTP material with an emission center at 595 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 549.69 ms [49]. Meanwhile, Guo et al. synthesized CDs/PVP film that demonstrates color tunability from colorless to brown and dynamic afterglow variation with a 253 ms lifetime, attributed to oxygen-triggered triplet exciton quenching and FRET from pristine CDs to photoactivated radicals [50]. Although the one-step CDs-based PRET system has been extensively studied and has achieved significant progress, the development of cascade PRET is still in its infancy, leaving the full exploitation of capabilities largely unexplored. The challenge remains in broadening the phosphorescence modulation range and maximizing the emission wavelength to meet the demands of information detection and anticounterfeiting applications.

Herein, we synthesized highly emissive CDs composites and developed broadly tunable afterglow CDs/PVA hybrid films through cascade FRET. By encapsulating the green CDs composites as the energy donor and Sulforhodamine 101 (SR101), Rhodamine B or Rhodamine 6G as the acceptor within the PVA films, we tuned the afterglow color of the hybrid films from green to red with broadband spectrum due to the efficient FRET between the two moieties. Further, we sequentially incorporated SR101 and oxazine 170 with RTP CDs composites in PVA substrates and significantly broadened the afterglow spectrum to the near-infrared (NIR) region owing to the relayed cascade FRET. The high efficiency and flexible FRET configurations of RTP CDs with various luminescent molecules give the CDs-based films tunable multicolor and broadband afterglow properties, thus facilitating their applications in fingerprint recognition and anticounterfeiting applications.

2-Hydroxy-1-naphthoic acid (PHA) and boric acid (BA) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sulforhodamine 101 (SR101), oxazine 170, rhodamine B (RhB), rhodamine 6G (Rh6G), and perchlorate were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All of the reagents were used without further purification

CDs are simply prepared by a one-step melting method. Firstly, BA and PHA powders are mixed in mass ratios of 3000:1, 600:1, and 3:1. The mixture was well ground at room temperature to obtain homogeneous white powders. Then the mixtures were transferred into a beaker and heated to melt in an oven at 180℃ for 10 min. After the reaction was finished and cooled to room temperature, CDs composites embedded in BA were collected and named CD1, CD2, and CD3, respectively. CD1 will be consistently referred to as CDs unless specified otherwise.

Firstly, PVA (3.5 g) was dissolved in deionized water (50 mL) and heated at 95 °C for 3 h. The obtained PVA aqueous solution (70 mg/mL) after filtration was used for the subsequent experiments. For the preparation of green CDs hybrid films, 50 mg of CDs was dispersed in deionized water (5 mL) and stirred for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of CDs aqueous solution was added to 2 ml PVA aqueous solution. The mixed solution was subjected to ultrasonication for 3 h. The green phosphorescence CDs/PVA films were fabricated using these solutions on 75 × 75 mm glass substrates, followed by 10 h of drying at 80 °C.

For the preparation of red CDs-based films, 1, 2, or 4 mg of SR101(or 20 mg of RhB, or 20 mg of Rh6G) was dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) and stirred for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of green phosphorescent CDs aqueous solution was added to 1 ml of SR101 aqueous solution, and the resulting mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 3 h. Subsequently, an appropriate amount of the sample was taken and mixed with a double volume of PVA solution. The preparation of multicolor CDs-PVA hybrid films for cascade FRET was similar to the process described above. The only variation was that the co-dissolved 1, 3, or 5 mg of Oxazine 170 and 2 mg of SR101 in 10 mL of ethanol were subjected to ultrasonic processing for 30 min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained on JEOL JEM-F200 at 200 kV. The PL spectra and photoluminescence quantum efficiency were measured using an Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–vis absorption was taken on a Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent Inc.). All the afterglow spectra were acquired at a 10 ms delay time after turning off the UV lamp. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on a VERTEX 70 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer. The afterglow lifetime profiles of the phosphorescent CDs and multicolor CDs/dye/PVA films were measured on an Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The dynamics traces were fitted using a multi-exponential function (Eq. (1)) with the deconvolution of an instrument response function of <600 ps.
(1)
I
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
A i
e x p
( -
t
τ i
)
where I(t) denotes the luminescence signal over time at a specific wavelength, τi and A i represent the lifetime and relative amplitude of a specific component.

The absolute PL QYs (Φ PL) of all-solid-state composites were measured using an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere under ambient conditions. The phosphorescence (Φ Phos) and photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (Φ PL) of the composites were obtained from the following Eq. (2):
(2)
Φ
Phos
= B / C ×
Φ
PL
where C and B represent the integral areas of total PL and phosphorescence spectra, respectively, and the phosphorescence was extracted from PL in a time-gated scheme.

In addition to confining in a rigid matrix to stabilize the triplet excited states, increasing the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) provides a more intrinsic approach for activating and enhancing the phosphorescence of PL materials. By integrating both (π-π*) and (n-π*) configurations, the significant overlap of transition orbitals facilitates the rapid population of the T1 state and enhances phosphorescence. Following this strategy, we selected the PHA molecule with rich hydroxyl and carboxyl groups as the precursor for synthesizing RTP CDs. Fig. 1 a depicts the synthesis of CDs composites and CDs-PVA hybrid films. By leveraging the solidification capabilities of BA, we prepared green emissive CDs composites with high quantum yields to serve as the RTP donor platforms for subsequent FRET processes. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs composites demonstrate persistent green PL for about 20 s after ceasing the light irradiation, indicating the PL properties associated with the triplet states and an afterglow feature (refer to Video S1). By employing a standard method [51], we quantified the PL and phosphorescence quantum yield (QY) of three CDs composites with different ratios of BA and PHA in the preparation. For the green afterglow CDs composites, CD1 exhibited the highest PL QY of 33.1 % and a phosphorescence QY of 21.7 % (Table S1). Fig. 1b shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs composites. The absorption around 250 nm is mainly attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C bonds, and the broadband from 300 to 400 nm is primarily ascribed to the n-π* transition of C=O bonds [23]. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the CDs emit a blue/green photoluminescence with dual emission bands around 400 nm and 525 nm. Notably, the emission at 525 nm demonstrates an afterglow feature once the excitation light is switched off (Fig. 1c and Fig. S1). The blue band aligns with the band gap energy (Eg) as determined from the Tauc plot, which was identified as fluorescence from the singlet states. The excitation PL spectrum (PLE) indicates that the blue emission is closely associated with the sub-fluorophore groups (C=O). Fig. 1c and Fig. S2 give the excitation and emission spectra of the green band for three CDs composites and demonstrate an almost identical excitation maximal around 360 nm and the afterglow emission maximal at 525 nm, suggesting that the RTP of these CDs originates from the identical surface state. The similarity of PLE spectra of blue and green emissions indicates that both singlet blue emission and triplet green RTP are closely related to the same moieties (C=O) of CDs composites. Additionally, the afterglow duration of CD1 at 120 °C is significantly reduced to approximately 8 s from 20 s at 20 °C (Fig. S3), further suggesting the phosphorescence afterglow rather than thermally activated delayed fluorescence. Thus, it can be inferred that the introduction of C=O groups into the CDs composites reduces the energy gap (ΔEst) between the lowest singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) states, thereby promoting the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and enhancing the generation of triplet excitons.

To gain insights into the persistent PL of CDs, we measured their time-resolved afterglow spectra and calculated the average phosphorescence lifetime of the three CDs composites by fitting their decay curves (Fig. 1d and Fig. S4) with single exponential function (Table S2). The average lifetime was calculated using equation (3), which is also applicable to the subsequent lifetime calculations.
(3)
τ
avg
= ∑
A i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
A i
τ i
where A i and τi represent the relative ratio in amplitude and lifetime of a specific component. The average lifetimes of three CDs composites were calculated to be 0.96 s for CD3, 1.05 s for CD2, and 1.04 s for CD1, respectively. The afterglow features were observed even by the naked eye in 7, 15, and 16 s, respectively (Fig. 1e; Videos S1-S3). As expected, the polycrystalline structure formed by the heat treatment with boric acid (BA) inhibits nonradiative transitions and shields the triplet excited state (T1) from oxygen, thereby enhancing the afterglow associated with C=O groups. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates for the PL and afterglow of CDs composites were determined to be (0.31, 0.53), (0.31, 0.54) and (0.31,0.54), respectively, corresponding to an actual green color that matches the observed emissions. The similarity in CIE chromaticity coordinates of CD1, CD2, and CD3 composites suggests that the quantity of the carbon source slightly affects the formation of PL centers and spectral profiles (Fig. S5), but the C=O groups play a dominant contribution to the enhanced ISC and RTP efficiency.

We utilized CD1 composite as a model to elucidate the phosphorescence generation mechanism. The TEM image in Fig. 2 a reveals that the CD1 is well-encapsulated and uniformly dispersed within the B2O3 matrix. HR-TEM image confirms the crystalline nature of CD1 with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite (inset of Fig. 2a) [6]. The particle size distribution, as shown in the histogram, ranges from 1.54 to 5.54 nm, with an average particle size of 3.30 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). The XRD spectra of the CD1, CD2, and CD3 composites show very similar patterns with three representative peaks at 14.4°, 28°, and 39.8°, which is mainly from the B2O3 crystal structure (Fig. S6). The surface functional groups of the CD1, CD2, and CD3 composites were investigated by FTIR measurements. As shown in Fig. 2b, all three CDs composites show broad absorption peaks around 3000–3700 cm−1, suggesting the presence of –OH groups on the CDs surface. These peaks exhibit a notable shift to lower wavenumbers from CD3 to CD1 due to the increased hydrogen bonding with C=O groups, which is consistent with the observed evolution in green afterglow properties. The absorption peaks at 1450 cm−1,1196 cm−1, and 960 cm−1 correspond to B-O, B-OH and B-C bonds, respectively, suggesting the covalent bonds between the CDs and the B2O3 matrix. The absorption peaks near 2952 and 1618 cm−1 are attributed to C–H and C=O bond vibrations [23,52]. The synergy of B-C covalent bonds, the rigid network structure, and hydrogen bonding between B2O3 and CDs stabilizes the triplet excited states of CDs and suppresses the non-radiative relaxation. Comparatively, the increased C=O groups reduce the ΔEst and promote the ISC, thus enhancing the phosphorescence of CD1.

XPS measurements were further conducted on CD1, CD2, and CD3 composites. As shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S7, all three samples exhibit peaks at approximately 193.0, 284.9, and 533.7 eV, corresponding to boron, carbon, and oxygen elements, respectively. Their C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d and Fig. S8) exhibit three components: C–C/C=C at 284.8 eV, C-O at 286.3 eV, and C=O at 288.8 eV. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2e and Fig. S9) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 532.6, 533.3, and 534.5 eV, confirming the presence of C=O, B-O, and C-O groups. The B 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2f) of CD1 shows three peaks at approximately 193.1 eV, 193.7 eV, and 195.0 eV, corresponding to BCO2, B2O3 and B-O bonds, respectively [32,51,53]. The presence of BCO2 and B-O bonds is also corroborated for CD2 and CD3 composites (Table S3), indicating that electron-withdrawing B atoms are covalently integrated into the CDs surface. These incorporated B atoms are crucial for modulating the phosphorescence due to their ability to attract π electrons of C=O groups within the CDs composites, thereby reducing the energy gap (ΔEST). Consequently, CD1 composite exhibits a relatively high RTP owing to the efficient ISC process with a small ΔEST value of 0.7 eV.

The above results and analyses shed light on the understanding of afterglow properties for the CDs composites. The generated CDs with emissive centers associated with C=O and O–H functional groups are embedded in a three-dimensional network of BA matrix through both covalent and hydrogen bonds, which prohibit the intermolecular motion of CDs and stabilize their triplet excitons. The incorporation of electron-withdrawing boron (B) atoms narrows the energy gap between singlet states and triplet states, boosting the density of triplet excitons and RTP efficiency.

Although the as-prepared CDs composites show highly green RTP and afterglow properties, the relatively narrow emission band limits their practical applications. FRET has been proven an effective approach to extend the emission spectrum of CDs-based afterglow materials [5]. Considering the good dispersibility in solution and abundant hydrogen bonding capacity, PVA was selected as a substrate to fabricate CDs-based afterglow films and CDs/dyes/PVA hybrid films with broadened afterglow spectrum through single or cascade FRET. As is known, triplet-to-singlet FRET can take place if the RTP spectrum of CDs as the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of PL molecules as the acceptor. This strategy is also expected in the sequential FRET by incorporating the RTP CDs with more fluorescence guests to tune the afterglow spectrum of CDs-based hybrid systems. To validate this concept, the as-prepared RTP CDs were embedded within a PVA matrix and the fabricated CDs/PVA film still exhibits green phosphorescence and afterglow features (Fig. S10). Compared to those of CDs composites, very similar optical properties are obtained including a phosphorescence spectrum with a peak at 525 nm, an average lifetime of 0.64 s (Figs. S11 and Fig. S12), a PL QY of 32.3 %, and a phosphorescence QY of 19.7 %. Additionally, the CDs composites were also dispersed in water and ethanol to prepare CDs/PVA films, as well as incorporated into polyacrylamide (PAM) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) (Fig. S13). The results indicate the CDs/PVA films with afterglow properties can be readily constructed with the CDs solution, suggesting good adaptability and flexibility of the CDs composites. On the other hand, CDs/PAM film demonstrates a relatively weakened phosphorescence and afterglow than the CDs/PVA film. The disappeared afterglow of CDs in PEG might be related to its distinct structure that could not provide strong enough hydrogen bonding interaction with CDs to stabilize the triplet excitons [47].

In the construction of the cascade FRET system of CDs as the primary donor, SR101 was selected as the red acceptor for triplet-singlet energy transfer, and Oxazine 170 as the NIR acceptor for the subsequent singlet–singlet energy transfer. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there is considerable spectral overlap between the phosphorescence spectrum of the CDs/PVA and the absorption spectra of fluorescent SR101, while the emission spectrum of SR101 aligns well with the absorption spectrum of the NIR fluorescent Oxazine 170, suggesting the potential for generating multicolor afterglow emissions through single and multiple FRET. Fig. 3b shows the schematic diagram of the involved FRET processes among CDs, SR101 and Oxazine 170 in regulating the afterglow color and spectrum of CDs-based hybrids. For the single stage FRET from CDs to SR101, the RTP CDs as the donor will transfer the radiation energy through dipole–dipole interaction to the acceptor SR101, resulting in the RTP quenching of CDs and the excitation/emission of SR101. Upon the UV excitation, the original green afterglow of CDs/PVA film is thus extended to red afterglow for CDs/SR101/PVA film. To assess the energy transfer process from the donor's triplet state to the acceptor, we measured the phosphorescence spectra of CDs/SR101/PVA films prepared with different concentrations of SR101. As shown in Fig. 3c, the green phosphorescence intensity of the CDs (10 mg/ml) gradually decreases with increasing the SR101 concentrations from 0.1 to 0.4 mg/mL, due to the absorption by SR101. Simultaneously, the primary afterglow peak undergoes a progressive redshift from 525 nm to 615 nm. This observation confirms the occurrence of triplet-singlet FRET between CDs and SR101. The intensity ratio of the two afterglow peaks at 615 nm and 525 nm can be regulated by changing the contents of SR101 from I615/I525 = 0.26 to I615/I525 = 3.54, thus the afterglow color and spectral profile are significantly extended to red region.

To further explore and optimize the energy transfer efficiency, we measured the phosphorescence decay curves of CDs/SR101/PVA films at 525 nm with varying SR101 concentrations (0.1–0.4 mg/ml) (Fig. 3d). Exponential fitting of these curves indicates that the phosphorescence lifetime of the donor (CDs) decreases progressively from 641 ms to 286.2 ms. Therefore, the FRET efficiencies between the CDs and SR101 were determined by the following equation:
(4)
Φ
FRET
= 1 -
τ
DA
/
τ D
where
τ
D A
and
τ D
are the phosphorescence lifetimes of CDs/SR101/PVA and CDs/PVA, respectively. The FRET efficiency is increased with increasing the contents of SR101 and reaches a maximum of 55.3 % at the concentration of 0.4 mg/ml (Fig. 3e), thus the corresponding FRET rate is 1.93 s−1. As a consequence, the afterglow intensity and duration visible to the naked eye decrease as Φ FRET increases. These results substantiate the successful establishment of an efficient FRET system between the donor RTP CDs and the acceptor SR101, with the tunable afterglow color through modulating SR101 concentrations. Moreover, we calculated the energy transfer rate (k FRET) according to the following equations [49,54]:
(5)
k
FRET
=
1
τ D
1
R
0
6
κ
2
φ D
9 ln
10
128
π
5
n
4
N A
J
( λ )
(6)

=
0.02108 (
κ
2
φ D
n
- 4
J
( λ )
)
1 / 6
(7)
J
( λ )
=
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
ε A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ
/
[
∫
0
∞
F D
( λ )
d λ ]
where τ D is the emission lifetime of the donor, κ 2 is the orientation factor,
φ D
is the quantum yield of the donor, J(λ) is the spectrum overlap integration, and n is the refractive index of the medium. F D(λ) means the area-normalized emission spectrum of the donor, and ε A(λ) is the molar absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Therefore, the k FRET can be calculated to be 1.26 s−1, which is in good agreement with the FRET rate from lifetime measurements. The distance (R 0) between CDs and SR101 is around 3.36 nm. The slow rate and large spacing can exclude the contribution of charge transfer between CDs and SR101 in the hybrid films.

Additionally, the adaptability of CDs to fabricate the FRET system with other luminescent molecules was evaluated by replacing SR101 with Rh6G and RhB as the acceptors. Both CDs/Rh6G/PVA and CDs/RhB/PVA films exhibit different afterglow colors but broadened afterglow spectra (Fig. S14, Fig. S15), indicating the occurrence of triplet-singlet FRET in these hybrids. The CDs/Rh6G/PVA film shows a yellow afterglow with a lifetime of 260 ms at 600 nm, while the CDs/RhB/PVA films display a red afterglow with a lifetime of 470 ms at 640 nm (Table S4). These observations not only confirm the successful implementation of FRET in these donor–acceptor pairs but also illustrate the universally applicable RTP CDs for constructing color-tunable afterglow hybrid systems.

Furthermore, Oxazine 170 was incorporated into the above FRET system to achieve cascade FRET and extend the afterglow spectrum to the NIR region. As presented in Fig. 3a, the RTP spectrum of CDs/SR101/PVA aligns well with the absorption spectrum of Oxazine 170, indicating the feasibility of a sequential FRET in the CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA film. As expected, the emission intensity around 655 nm is steadily increased with increasing the contents of Oxazine 170 (Fig. 4 a–c), suggesting a relayed singlet–singlet FRET from SR101 to Oxazine 170, in addition to the triplet-singlet FRET from CDs to SR101. Accordingly, this cascade FRET endows the CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films with a persistent afterglow in the ultrabroad spectral ranges from green to NIR (Fig. 4d–f), but distinct spectral profiles and afterglow colors due to different contents of acceptor Oxazine 170. The singlet–singlet FRET is also evidenced by the reduced fluorescence lifetime of SR101 at 610 nm from 4.32 ns to 2.39 ns when 0.5 mg/ml of Oxazine 170 was introduced (Fig. 4g), corresponding to a k FRET of 1.87 × 108 s−1. The FRET efficiencies were calculated from the lifetimes of SR101 with changing the contents of Oxazine 170, and displayed in Fig. 4h. A maximal efficiency of 44.7 % is achieved, confirming the efficient cascade FRET processes in this CDs-based hybrid film. The k FRET was also calculated from Eqs. (5)–(7) to be 1.86 × 108 s−1, which is well consistent with the above value. Similarly, the distance between SR101 and Oxazine 170 was estimated to be 4.62 nm. It is worth noting that there is a limited extent of spectral overlap between the phosphorescence of CDs and the absorption of Oxazine 170, but this insufficient spectral overlap impedes the direct triplet-singlet FRET to Oxazine 170 and thus has little contribution to the NIR afterglow (Fig. S16).

These results indicate that the highly emissive RTP CDs can be utilized as the primary energy donor to construct tunable and multicolor afterglow CDs-based hybrid films by incorporating various luminescent molecules to achieve efficient single-step and cascade FRET. The tunable afterglow colors of CDs/SR101/PVA films and CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films can be also demonstrated in their CIE coordinate diagrams (Fig. 4i) and images under/after the UV excitations (Fig. 4j). For CDs/SR101/PVA films with different contents of SR101, the CIE coordinates can be changed from (0.38, 0.42) to (0.52, 0.4), and different afterglow colors change from green, yellow, orange, to red with the naked eye. With the introduction of different contents of Oxazine 170, the cascade FRET further regulates the CIE coordinates of CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA films from (0.58, 0.35) to (0.47, 0.31), and their afterglow colors are extended to deep red because of their broadened spectra reaching NIR region. Therefore, it can be expected that these CDs-based hybrids with tunable and ultrabroad afterglow properties will possess potential applications in many fields.

The exceptional afterglow properties of these color-tunable RTP CDs-based systems inspire us to explore their potential applications in fingerprint detection, data encryption, and luminescent ink [55]. In the realm of criminal identification, fingerprint detection is a crucial element that frequently encounters difficulties due to the lack of optical contrast against various backgrounds. To address this, phosphorescence materials are preferred for their ability to minimize luminescent background interference. First of all, we employed a powder coating method to develop latent fingerprints, capitalizing on the effective mechanical adhesion of CDs/PVA powder to the oily components of fingerprint residues, which are not readily visible under daylight. Excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp causes impurities on the glass to emit a bright blue emission that can interfere with the PL of fingerprint images. However, once the UV light is switched off, the background fluorescence fades away, leaving a distinct green phosphorescence image of the fingerprint with discernible bifurcations and loops (Fig. 5 a). Therefore, the RTP CDs/PVA hybrids demonstrate excellent abilities in latent fingerprint detection. Furthermore, the multi-level anticounterfeiting and information encryption capabilities of CDs-based hybrid systems were evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 5b, advanced security patterns utilizing both fluorescence and RTP were presented. The petals and coded numbers 1–4 were composed of CDs/Rh6G/PVA, CDs/PVA, CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA, and CDs/RhB/PVA hybrids, respectively. Under UV illumination, the patterns exhibit a vibrant multicolor afterglow, serving as the security protection mode. When turning off the UV lamp, petals 3–4 display distinct orange and red RTP contrasting with those under UV excitation, while petals 1–2 show yellow and green afterglow that closely match their colors under prompt PL. In the initial short periods after turning off the UV light, multiple color combinations of “SOISI” code can be observed, serving as the first-level encrypted message. After three seconds, the letter “I” disappears, leading to a reorganization of the original code to “SOIS”. As the delay reaches five seconds later, the second-level encrypted message is further transformed into “SS”, demonstrating multiple levels of information encryption. Moreover, the CDs-based hybrids can also be used as security ink to create versatile patterns for anti-counterfeiting through writing. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the handwritten message “101” was crafted using a complex solution of CDs/PVA, CDs/SR101/PVA, and CDs/SR101/Oxazine170/PVA, respectively. Under the excitation of a UV lamp, the numeric character exhibits diverse colors due to the intrinsic afterglow of CDs and the modulated afterglow after single or cascade FRET of CDs/dyes/PVA hybrids. Once the UV light is switched off, these numbers show differentially varying multicolor afterglow that is readily visible to the naked eye. These results demonstrate that the CDs-based hybrids with tunable broadband afterglow and flexible preparation can serve as promising candidates for latent fingerprint detection and anticounterfeiting coding, as well as writing sensitive documents for security purposes.

In summary, we have synthesized green RTP CDs composites with a high phosphorescence QY of 21.7 % and a lifetime of 1.05 s. The introduction of C=O groups into the preparation of CDs composites can effectively reduce the energy gap between the singlet and triplet states to promote the ISC process and enhance the phosphorescence. The as-prepared RTP CDs demonstrate good adaptability and flexibility in constructing CDs-based afterglow films, which is conducive to their practical applications. Particularly, the RTP CDs can be combined with a variety of luminescent dyes to construct CDs/dyes/PVA systems with single- and cascade FRET among the involved moieties. Accordingly, tunable multicolor and broadband afterglow of CDs-based hybrids have been successfully achieved and extended as needed. The afterglow spectra of the CDs/RS101/Oxazine170/PVA film can cover the large region from green to NIR. As examples of applications, the CDs-based hybrids demonstrate the promising potential for advanced anticounterfeiting and fingerprint detection. Our findings are significant for developing CDs-based hybrids with multicolor and broadband afterglow properties through cascade FRET strategy.

Zhanpeng Liu: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiangbo Ding: Visualization, Investigation. Lin Cai: Methodology, Data curation. Yihan Jia: Visualization, Methodology. Xia Ran: Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhongzheng Guo: Methodology, Investigation. Dan Mo: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yatao Pan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Lijun Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.160311.
